item.dc:creator: CodyBurkett

item.title: We repaired our 100 year old 'hardwood' Douglas Fir floor. I'm sore.
item.description:
The stratigraphy of a 100 year old floor.
Nails and hammer marks. They tried to get it to stop squeaking. It still squeaks. It will always squeak. It's 100 years old. Though this was probably done in the 60s, the decade of leaded gasoline, asbestos and arguably successful communism. about ~20 work hours of punching nails down. about ~10 work hours of filling those holes with putty followed by bonus sanding.
The dust that lies beneath your disgusting, filthy carpet.
2 days of sanding, $150 of sandpaper, $150 for the rental. Did you know that sufficient quantities of sawdust is capable of self-igniting and even exploding? https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8840308/ https://publications.iafss.org/publications/fss/1/463/view/fss_1-463.pdf
We slept on it between sanding weekend and finish weekend, doing edges and minor repairs throughout.
The edger we rented was NFG so I used a belt sander I got at a yard sale for $20 to do the edges and then lots of aftercare with finer orbital grits. I did a little patch there at the vent. $50 of fir just for that and the other patch in the background.
After 1.5 days of applying floor polyurethane. I wanted to use the enthusiast stuff (Bona, Loba) but it was difficult to find here in Canada in my time-frame so I used hardware store stuff and it worked well. They are very runny and it reminded me a bit of that robot that tries to scoop up it's own hydraulic fluid.
I bag polished coats 1 and 2 and hand sanded the 3rd of the 4 light coats I applied because I'm too cheap and lazy to drive the hour to a rental place with a buffer. My wife and I polished the final product with some brown paper bags after it was dry but before it had too much time to cure. We have to keep furniture off it for at least a few days, though we may move the bed back tomorrow so we don't have to sleep in the yard. We spent about 40 hours of my personal labour and 60 hours including family helpers and my spouse. I spent about $1000 on consumables, including the tool rental, sand paper, polyurethane finish and gas. I think conservatively that this floor could raise the value of my very old and modest house by around $10,000. I learned a lot through this process and I mostly enjoyed it, though the repetitive physical motions did injure me more than once and I'm still hurting as I write this. If you ever have any questions on taking on a project like this yourself, feel free to ask and I can let you know more about what I learned doing this and where you can learn more.
item.dc:creator: HumansArePrettyNeat

item.title: Bad behavior starts at home
item.description: I work with abused children and children with behavioral issues. Children with behavioral issues usually have parents who aren't overtly abusive, but are very dismissive of their children's needs. Often the only reason therapy even works with children is because it gives them a friend who will genuinely listen when the rest of their family won't. This isn't always the case, but notice I'm not wording any of this as if it is. It's just, at least in my experience, almost always caused by bad parenting. Not necessarily overtly abusive, but just not listening, not paying attention, and just dismissing the kid's issues as "mental illness" and being dismissive about that. Obviously when it's something more neurological like Autism Spectrum, ADHD, etc it's not bad parenting, but they're also not the majority of the kids that need actual therapy. I'm just so tired of having to play nice with bad parents.
item.dc:creator: BerryBnuuy

item.title: Returning stolen goods pt 58
item.dc:creator: MrsPrendsen

item.title: Got the job and I start soon!!!
item.description: I was let go a couple months ago and had since applied for hundreds of jobs... Initially I was so depressed being unemployed and worrying about not bringing in an income to help my family. One of the first jobs I applied for was just amazing. It was a long shot but I really wanted it so I thought I'd give it a try. If I don't apply, I don't even give them the option to say no, right? I kept applying to other jobs but eventually that first job got back to me and wanted to interview me. Grueling interviews, but I got through them all and I can't believe it. I got the job!!!!! Woohoo!!!!!!!!!!!!

item.dc:creator: Unholytrashpanda

item.title: Pinch me, I must be dreaming...
item.description: I worked on a software product for eight years, ending in 2022. About 50% of my job was development, and 50% was support. I loved the job, but I hated the new overlords that bought our small company. I get a call a week ago from a company looking to hire someone part-time to support this product for their customers. I was not even aware there were third party companies contracting out support. So, this new job is pretty much doing the 50% of my job that was support. This new gig pays less than I make now, but still easily livable. They say it should never be more than 16 hours a week, but as an example, in the entire month of June so far, they have had SIX hours of work. I get paid regardless. In fact, if I work more than 16 hours in a week, I get paid more. They also don't care if I have a regular full time job, as long as I can respond to issues within the contracted time window. I do not think I will be keeping my full-time job as it makes me miserable; but I may try to get something completely different, like a gig bartending in a brewery or something. I keep thinking I'll wake up and this will be a cruel dream.

item.title: To the drag queen that bullied me into another mimosa AND gave me prop production tips, you da real mvp.
item.description: Turns out to make a fairly convincing faux metal finish, UV nail gel and metallic powder will make a FANTASTIC near mirror shine. Here's hoping v2 ends up looking a little less rough hewn. I've tried everything from paints (couldn't find alclad near me) to electroplating, but this is the only thing I can definitively say will finish a print in less than 5 minutes with good results. Does anyone however know how to kill the glitter effect down a bit?

item.title: 4 Years of Solar Power
item.description:
I've now had my solar net metering system up and running for 4 full years, patterns have begun to emerge. To see more details about the installation and first year, click here: https://imgur.com/gallery/year-of-solar-power-o9hyYrQ To see more details about the second year, click here: https://imgur.com/gallery/two-years-of-solar-power-m6lMmVo To see more details about the third year, click here: https://imgur.com/gallery/3-years-of-solar-power-82tdwz0 Edit: My system is located in South-West Ontario, one of the best spots in Canada for solar power.
Cat Tax: Merlin Edit: I swear I put this picture last, I see Imgur hasn't changed completely.
Pretty crappy day for solar power today, but luckily that has not often been the case lately.
I'll start with the data I found most interesting, and what most people want to know first anyway, the money. Now that I've had several years worth of summer's to build up credits, and then winter's to spend them, a clear pattern has developed in my electrical bills. It seems every year beginning in March/April I start accumulating enough generation that my electrical bill drops to the minimum ~$27 that the utility can charge me. These credits run out in January, then the cycle begins again in March/April. As you can see this means my electrical bill is reduced by about 60% most of the year, and only 27% in those winter months when I'm all out of credits.
Here are the averages in graph form. I'm fully expecting these to lower further as more years of data come in and the $27 bill becomes the norm.
Here is the updated graph of how much I am generating on average each month.
And the upgraded net electrical consumption graph. As you can see March - June I am generating more than I am consuming, this is where those credits are generated that I then reap the benefits of in Fall.
The electrical prices are back on the increase, seems the COVID-era price break is finally truly gone.
Between the increase in electrical prices, and the lower overall amounts I have been paying out recently, I am now projecting pay-back in 2029-2030.
Graph-break, check out my cool vegetable/herb garden. My dad made these planter boxes himself. Here's a cool coffee table he made: https://imgur.com/gallery/hand-made-coffee-table-dq063gN
Windows HDR is going to make these a bit hard to read, blame Microsoft. Here is how much my utility company records that I am consuming.
And here is how much they think I am generating. Note that they can't get an accurate number here, since they can't record what I am generating and using before it reaches the meter.
Speaking of the meter, here it is along with the big off switch that has never been flipped. The meter spins the normal way when I'm importing energy from the grid, and spins the other way when I'm exporting to the grid.
And here is the Net difference that the utility company is recording on that bi-directional meter.
Finally, here is the actual amount of solar energy I'm producing as recorded by my monitoring device.
Here is how much each of my panels/microinverters has generated so far. Very consistant 1.27 MWh on the back of the house, and a more varied 500 - 700 kWh on the front of the house (due to trees and being further from true-south).
Total daily generation since the system was turned on. This makes the Summer peaks and Winter valleys more obvious. At least it would if it weren't for Windows HDR screenshots.
Here's another view from the monitoring software, this includes the feel-good equivalency numbers. So far my system has generated 26.86 megawatt-hours of electricity, which according to them is equivalent to: -235.9 years of powering a light bulb -33.3 flights from New York to LA in CO2 offset -833.3 years of powering an average phone -16.3 years of powering a refrigerator -485 trees planted in CO2 offset -11 nights of powering the lighting of the Eiffel Tower -15.8 barrels of crude oil in energy equivalency -1,190.7 laps at the Monaco Grand Prix
item.dc:creator: GeneralMuffinMan

item.title: If you die tomorrow
item.description: A friend tried to commit suicide. I feel conflicted at the moment. Wishing she had succeeded, as she would've gotten the peace she deserved, but also hating myself for wanting this. I tried finding the artist, but couldn't find who made this. I'll update later. Edit: Pascal Campion
item.dc:creator: Kantoken
